title: Triage queue with a hard capacity cap
problem: Without a hard limit on concurrent work, a side-practice consultant accepts every engagement that looks 'doable' and ends up with three clients at once eating evenings and weekends — and family-first, the value the whole practice is built to protect, quietly falsifies. This feature makes the load-bearing rule structural rather than aspirational: one active build plus one in triage, no more. The homepage shows the current state so a visitor knows immediately whether a slot is open, and the server refuses to create a new engagement past the cap. The public waitlist is not a flaw to hide — scarcity reads as seriousness. This is the single rule of the portal that does not bend.
analysis: The cap is enforced where it cannot be wished away — in the server. The current load is not a number someone maintains by hand; it is counted directly from the engagement records, so it is always true and it decrements on its own when an engagement ships. A public endpoint reports whether the practice is at capacity, and the homepage hero turns that into a plain-language slot indicator. When a visitor submits an intake while at capacity, the server declines to create a new engagement and the visitor is routed to a waitlist acknowledgement instead. Crucially there is no override: no admin toggle raises the cap, because the rule that must not bend should not have a soft switch in the code.

decisions.decision: Hard cap of one active build plus one in triage, with no exception path
decisions.rationale: This is the bedrock defence. If it breaks, family-first collapses — three concurrent clients against side-practice hours consumes weekends. The failure pattern it structurally prevents is the optimism of 'I can squeeze this one in'.
decisions.recommendation: Enforce it server-side, not as a soft warning, and provide no override anywhere in the admin surface.

decisions.decision: The public capacity indicator is an asset, not a bug to hide
decisions.rationale: Scarcity adds credibility. A visible 'currently full, waitlist open' positions the consultant as serious rather than unavailable, and the waitlist itself becomes acquisition infrastructure.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the indicator on the homepage hero and let it read straight from real engagement counts so it is never stale.

decisions.decision: Current load is derived from engagement records, never hand-maintained
decisions.rationale: A hand-edited capacity figure drifts the moment someone forgets to update it after an engagement ships. Counting active and triage engagements directly makes the figure self-correcting and removes a standing source of error.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the cap thresholds as named constants next to the counting logic so raising them is a deliberate, reviewable code change.

implementation: The capacity primitive is server-side. functions/api/capacity.ts answers GET /api/capacity with the live active and triage counts, the cap thresholds, and an at-capacity flag. The counting and the cap constants (one active, one in triage) live in functions/_lib/sessions.ts and are derived from the engagement records in the database. When an intake is submitted, POST /api/sessions checks the same counts and answers with a 409 when a tier is full, so no engagement is created past the cap. On the client side, the homepage hero (src/components/Hero.tsx) fetches /api/capacity on mount and renders a slot indicator — an open slot, or 'full, waitlist open'. The intake form reads the same endpoint to set expectations before submission. There is deliberately no admin endpoint or toggle that raises the cap.
successCriteria:
- The active-plus-triage cap is enforced by the server, not by policy or a soft warning
- Submitting an intake at capacity is declined cleanly and routed to a waitlist acknowledgement
- The homepage shows the current capacity state, read from real engagement counts
- The current load decrements on its own when an engagement ships — nobody maintains it by hand
- There is no override path anywhere that can raise the cap
- Reusable as a pattern for any solo-operator service that wants the same structural rule
userGuide: The capacity state is computed automatically from engagement records — there is nothing to update by hand. GET /api/capacity returns the current counts and the at-capacity flag; the homepage hero and the intake form both read it. The one-active-plus-one-triage limit and the rule that submissions past it become waitlist entries are enforced inside the sessions endpoint. To change the cap, edit the thresholds in functions/_lib/sessions.ts — a deliberate code change, by design, with no UI shortcut.
knownLimitations: The capacity figure reflects engagement records only — it does not model a richer queue with per-slot start dates
The homepage indicator is fetched on page load, so a visitor with a stale tab sees the state from when they opened it until the next load
The waitlist is an acknowledgement state on a declined submission, not yet a managed list with visible queue positions

statusHistory.reason: Capacity gating live; homepage hero shows the slot state and the intake submit path respects the cap.

Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at functions/api/capacity.feature.json. Capacity is now derived live from engagement records via the server; the earlier hand-edited JSON file is gone.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial fill from the brainstorming session — the load-bearing capacity cap.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Built as a hand-edited capacity file read at build time, with a homepage counter component.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Code-first overhaul: capacity is now a server endpoint that counts real engagement records, so the feature.json is co-located at functions/api/capacity.feature.json and anchored on capacity.ts. Dropped every stale limitation — the hand-edited file, the manual decrement, the unwired waitlist, and the rebuild-to-update lag are all gone.
